records.ety: [OE. owen, awen, aghen, to have, own, have (to do), hence, owe, AS. āgan to have; akin to G. eigen, a., own, Icel. eiga to have, Dan. eie, Sw. äga, Goth. igan, Skr. īç. √110. Cf. Ought, v., 2d Own, Fraught.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Owed (ōd), (Ought (t) obs.); p. pr. & vb. n. Owing (ō"ng).]

defs: To possess; to have, as the rightful owner; to own.
quotes: Thou dost here usurp The name thou ow'st not.

defs: To have or possess, as something derived or bestowed; to be obliged to ascribe (something to some source); to be indebted or obliged for; as, he owed his wealth to his father; he owed his victory to his lieutenants.
quotes: O deem thy fall not owed to man's decree.

defs: To have or be under an obligation to restore, pay, or render (something) in return or compensation for something received; to be indebted in the sum of; as, the subject owes allegiance; the fortunate owe assistance to the unfortunate.
quotes: The one ought five hundred pence, and the other fifty.
A son owes help and honor to his father.
notes: ☞ Owe was sometimes followed by an objective clause introduced by the infinitive. “Ye owen to incline and bow your heart.”

defs: To have an obligation to (some one) on account of something done or received; to be indebted to; as, to owe the grocer for supplies, or a laborer for services.
